artifacts.content:
Hi Elena,

Kids are finally in bed and Daniel’s still on his laptop remoting into some server at Rochester Regional, so I’m grabbing a quiet minute.

You asked how we’re managing money-wise. Honestly, it’s this weird patchwork. Since March 2024, the ladies from my Wednesday afternoon support group at Wilmot Cancer Institute have been buying the chemo caps and prayer shawls I crochet during infusion days. Melissa, the oncology nurse, was the one who nudged me to start selling them instead of just giving them away. It’s usually only $80–$150 a month, but it covers gas and parking for Strong.

On top of that, remember how I told you about the Brighton High winter concert? In November and December 2024, Mrs. Keegan let me set up a little Willow Ridge Threads table in the auditorium lobby. I did school-colored hats and scarves and somehow made about $600 between cash and a few Venmo payments. Lucia was so proud, dragging her friends over to “my mom’s booth.”

And do you remember when I posted in the “Rochester Breast Cancer Warriors” group back in January–February 2024 about my Stage II diagnosis and trying to pay for the OmniSeq testing? I listed leftover hats on Facebook Marketplace and ended up with roughly $350. A few women even sent extra through Zelle, just saying they wanted to help me “fight this thing.”

It’s all tiny pieces, but they add up. I keep telling myself that once the fatigue eases, I might be able to do a couple of craft fairs again, or maybe some tutoring, just to feel like I’m contributing more than scarves and copay money.

Love you,
Carla

Hi Mami,

I wanted to say thank you again for everything you and Papi have been doing. I know you say it’s “nothing,” but it isn’t nothing to me.

Since April 2024, after Sunday Mass at Our Lady of Guadalupe, you and Dad keep slipping $100–$150 in cash into my hand and telling Father Ramirez it’s your “donation for Carla’s treatment.” I feel guilty taking it, but that money is what lets me put gas in the car for Wilmot and Strong and still buy groceries without panicking every time I open the fridge.

I’m also still paying off some of the extra cancer bills. The genetic counselor at the hereditary cancer clinic at Wilmot, Sarah Klein, was very kind in September, but her visit was $275, and the BRCA lab work left us with a $410 balance after insurance. We set up a $50 monthly payment plan starting in October 2024, so that’s another bill I track in my little binder.

On top of that, there are the mental health visits. I see Dr. Grant in Brighton twice a month, and now Dr. Li at Strong for the anxiety and insomnia. Between them and the medications, it feels like there’s always another statement in the mail.

Please don’t worry about us too much. Daniel’s job at Rochester Regional is steady, and the kids are okay—Lucia’s already talking about college visits again, and Evan is glued to his soccer ball as usual. Your caldo de pollo in the freezer and those “donations” after Mass make me feel less alone in all of this.

Te quiero mucho,
Carla

Kitchen notebook – Budget/health notes

Mental health:
Since March 2024 I’ve been seeing Dr. Karen Li (psychiatry) at Strong Memorial for the panic and insomnia that got worse after the breast cancer diagnosis. She changed my meds in June 2024, and between her visits and the new sleep/anxiety prescriptions, it’s running about $185/month out-of-pocket. Add that to the ~$120/month I already pay for therapy with Dr. Melissa Grant in Brighton, and we’re at roughly $305/month just for my brain.

Physical stuff:
Chemo and surgery left me with the lymphedema in my right arm, plus the lovely menopausal symptoms. In May 2024, Dr. Patel at Rochester Gynecology Associates started me on that non-formulary menopause med for the hot flashes and night sweats. Insurance barely touches it, so that’s another $165/month.

Pelvic floor PT at Lattimore Physical Therapy in Brighton with Jenna Morris since January 2024 for the incontinence issues from chemo: $95 per session, usually 2x/month → about $190/month.

Smaller things (mouthwash for sores, creams, stool softeners, etc.) I just pay as they come up at the pharmacy or Target. They’re mostly little charges, so I’ve never really tried to set up payment plans or ask for discounts on those; I just swipe the card and move on.

Reminder: update Daniel on totals before next payday so we can plan around Lucia’s college visit gas money and Evan’s soccer tournament fees.

To: Marisol

Hey Mari,

Sitting in the Wegmans parking lot trying to decide if I go in or just nap in the car, lol. Chemo-brain and hot flashes are tag-teaming me today.

Remember how I told you chemo wrecked my hormones? Back in May 2024, Dr. Patel at Rochester Gynecology Associates put me on this non-formulary menopause med for the night sweats. It does help, but it’s $165/month out of pocket. On top of that, I’m still doing pelvic floor PT at Lattimore in Brighton with Jenna Morris since January for the incontinence stuff—$95 a session, twice a month. My wallet cries every time I check in at the desk.

I just got a message that there’s a new prescription waiting at CVS from Dr. Li at Strong. I’m staring at the app trying to decide if I really need to pick it up now or if I can wait. I’ve started skipping anything that isn’t absolutely necessary, because I can’t keep adding more to the pile.

On the plus side, I made a little extra over the holidays babysitting for two of Daniel’s IT coworkers from Rochester Regional when their daycare shut down for RSV—about $300 in cash tips between Thanksgiving and New Year’s 2024. That helped with the OmniSeq bill and Christmas presents.

Anyway, enough money talk. How did your choir kids do at the winter concert? I miss that chaos.

– C

Hi Elena,

Quick update before I crash. Today was one of those “too many doctors, not enough Carla” days.

This morning I had pelvic floor PT with Jenna Morris at Lattimore in Brighton. She’s still optimistic about the progress since we started back in January 2024, even if I feel like I’m moving at snail speed. Then I drove over to Strong for my appointment with Dr. Li. We talked again about the panic that spikes every time a new bill shows up.

I still see Dr. Grant in Brighton twice a month. Between her, Dr. Li, and all the other specialists, it feels like my calendar is just names and copays. I know there are a couple of follow-up visits my oncologist recommended—one extra imaging study and a check-in with the cardiologist—but I’ve been dragging my feet on scheduling them. Part of it is fatigue, but honestly a big part is that I just can’t stomach adding more bills right now.

On the brighter side, Mom and Dad came over after Mass on Sunday with caldo de pollo and left another little “treatment donation” in my coat pocket. And Lucia drove me to PT today to get more practice behind the wheel; she’s already talking about Brockport and Geneseo visits this spring. Evan has a soccer scrimmage Saturday, so I’m trying to keep that day doctor-free.

Call me when you get a break from the floor. I want to hear about your new charge nurse.

Love,
Carla

Event: Brighton HS Winter Concert & Crochet Table
Date: 2024-12-12
Time: 5:30 pm – 9:00 pm
Location: Brighton High School auditorium lobby
Attendees: Carla, Daniel, Lucia, Evan

Notes:

Arrive by 5:30 pm so I can set up the Willow Ridge Threads table in the auditorium lobby before the doors open. Mrs. Keegan invited me again after seeing how well the Brighton-colored hats and scarves did in November 2024. Goal is to move the last of the blue-and-gold sets and maybe take a few custom orders.

Bring:
- Cash box with small bills
- Venmo QR code sign linked to my personal account
- Extra business cards with “Willow Ridge Threads” and email (cjmendoza76@gmail.com)
- Small mirror for people to try on hats

Lucia has to be backstage by 6:15 for warmup with choir; Daniel will run Evan home if he gets restless. Remind Lucy to swing by the table after the concert—she loves playing “sales assistant.”

Also: make a mental note to chat with a few of the parents about possible babysitting over the holidays like I did for Daniel’s IT coworkers at Rochester Regional last year (picked up about $300 in cash tips between Thanksgiving and New Year’s 2024 when their daycare closed for RSV).

If energy holds, crochet during slow parts so I have more chemo caps ready for the Wednesday Wilmot support group ladies on infusion days.

metadata.title: Brighton HS Winter Concert + sales table

artifacts.content:
Notebook – Early 2024 money/health snapshot

Jan–Feb 2024:
Posted leftover crocheted hats and scarves on Facebook Marketplace after sharing my Stage II diagnosis and the OmniSeq test situation in the “Rochester Breast Cancer Warriors” group. Made roughly $350 total. A few women sent extra via Zelle, saying it was to help me “fight this thing.” Used most of it toward OmniSeq invoice #8473 and the rest for gas to Strong.

March 2024 onward:
Started bringing chemo caps and prayer shawls to infusion days at Wilmot Cancer Institute at Strong. Melissa (oncology nurse) kept telling other Wednesday afternoon support group members about them, and now I usually bring a small bag each week. Extra income averages $80–$150 a month. Try to keep at least 5 neutral colors and 3 bright ones in stock.

Holiday 2024:
Thanksgiving through New Year’s 2024 – babysat for two families from Daniel’s IT department at Rochester Regional Health when their daycare closed because of RSV cases. About $300 in cash tips total. Helped with Christmas presents and one month of Dr. Grant’s therapy bill.

Ongoing:
Dad’s stroke in July 2024 at Rochester General → since August I’ve been pitching in about $120/month toward his PT and speech therapy co-pays at the Rochester Regional outpatient rehab so they don’t cut his sessions. Add this to my own oncology, mental health, PT, and gyn costs when I do the monthly budget check-in.

metadata.title: Side income + family medical help log
